title: vimeo_1194211057
displayTitle: 2026-05-28 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of Meridian Energy Limited
topic: Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of Meridian Energy Limited
description: 28 May 2026 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of Meridian Energy Limited Meridian Energy Limited Mike Roan, Chief Executive Mark Verbiest, Board Chairperson
descriptionSummary: Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of Meridian Energy Limited. Meridian Energy Limited. Mike Roan, Chief Executive

chunks.text: It's great to have you here. You are the third of our- Yep... Gen Tailers in front of us, so we look forward to the conversation. Can I just ask colleagues if we can, when we're asking questions, can we ask questions, not speeches? I will jump on you for that if they get too long because I know there'll be lots of questions that people want to ask, and that swallows up other people's time.

But what we will do is hand over to you to do the intros and to say what you want to say to start with for five minutes or so, and then we'll go to questions. Thank- I know there will be plenty. Thank you, Mr. Chair. I've got all afternoon. And we are aware that there's something else on today as well, obviously. Oh, but this is important.

Yes, it is. Absolutely. Tena koutou katoa. I'm Mark Verbeest. I'm the chair of Meridian Energy, and I've got with me Mike Rowan, who's our CEO. We will keep it brief because we genuinely do want you to get through your questions, and hopefully we can answer them all to your satisfaction.

You'll be aware we're the largest electricity generator, and we remain committed to generating electricity only from renewable sources. We own and operate seven hydro stations, six on the Waitaki, one in Manapouri, eight wind farms and a grid scale factory, and of course, we're building some more.

We also service roughly 460,000 customers presently through two brands, Meridian and Powershop. We know we've got a significant role to play in helping Kiwis power their homes and businesses to reduce overall energy costs and in growing and decarbonizing the economy.

And I want to assure the committee that we do take these responsibilities seriously. Our people are very committed and proud of the company and its assets, and rightly so. We were quite recently recognized by Standard and Poor's Global Dow Jones Best in Class as a global reference point for renewable energy.

We're actually the only New Zealand company in the world index and one of only 16 energy companies globally that is in this index in the top 10. And that is around measuring sustainability in all its forms. It's environmental, it's governance related, it's also social, and that relates to how we treat our customers and how we treat our staff.

We know our work benefits New Zealand more broadly. Around 80 cents in every dollar we pay in dividends to the government, which is $0.54 goes directly to the government in dividends and another $0.25 goes into KiwiSaver funds invested. We're also, obviously, one of New Zealand's largest taxpayers, and when you take into account tax, around two-thirds of every dollar we make goes to the government in either dividends or tax.

Financial year '25 was very challenging for us, reflecting the cost of maintaining system security through winter 2024, where we had low hydrology, much more reduced gas availability. And our balance sheet is strong enough, and we maintain it in a strong enough position so that we can actually respond.

I would say our results in FY25 show in stark form that we do not benefit from high wholesale prices at Meridian. We understand the importance of balancing the expectations of customers, shareholders and you, our policymakers and regulators.

And I would say that, being Murphy's Law, we bought expensive hedging through that period, which cost us dearly, because not long after we'd done that, it rained. So, we're pretty quickly out of the money. But the point is that we do these things because we're conscious of particularly our customer stakeholders and smoothing responses for them.

So look, I'll leave it there for me, and I'll quickly hand over to Mike. Thanks, mate. And then happy to answer your questions. Thank you. Yeah. Ngā mihi, Mark, and tena koutou. The New Zealand economy is working its way through what's a pretty material structural adjustment driven by the collapse of gas production that we've used for any number of years, many probably 40, 50 years, to provide abundant, affordable fuel for direct consumers of gas and for the electricity sector.

And the pricing impact of the collapse has been and remains incredibly challenging for everybody because it affects all gas users and our electricity customers. It's going to take years to overcomeAnd there are no silver bullets that we can see that are available to solve it. So, the first thing I want to acknowledge is the pain that people are facing.

New Zealanders want more affordable electricity, so do we. And I want to assure people that we're doing everything that we can to mitigate the impact of this structural issue, but it will take time to overcome. So, the best way of doing this, of course, is to continue to invest in renewable generation and the firming and flexibility that supports it.

And over the last five years, Merian's invested more than a billion dollars in new generation, and we have a further two billion planned over the next three. We're not alone in this. You would have heard from the others, as you mentioned, Chair, that across the sector, new generation's being delivered at rates that are setting historical precedents.

We're scaling faster than any time in New Zealand's history. And the investment coupled with firming solutions like Meridian's grid-scale battery at Ruakaka, the Huntly Strategic Energy Reserve, and the demand response agreement that we've got with Rio Tinto, and the aluminum smelter at Tiwai Point, means that the electricity sector has secured sufficient dry year insurance for the next decade.

As you'd expect, the investments that we're making, the scale of that investment's put downward pressure on forward electricity prices. They've moved pretty quickly. Since January this year, the cost of electricity is reflected by the market traders' future electricity prices for calendar years '27, '28, and '29 have dropped by over 25%.

So they've come off materially. In fact, they've fallen so far that over that time that the prices at Benmore are now in line with our internal fundamental forecast, sitting around $120 a megawatt hour. That said, they remain above the 70 to 80 bucks per megawatt hour that we have enjoyed historically, and for the foreseeable future, we believe that they'll remain at that level, so the $120 per megawatt hour. And we can explain that if you're interested.

So- I think we will ask that trajectory, so yeah. Yeah. So while building new generation's the best thing that we can do, there's more that we have to do, and we've invested in new technology to underpin products and services for our retail team to help manage costs for customers, and we're committed to our most vulnerable customers.

We talked to this last year, but we offer an energy wellbeing program that we've spent over 3 million bucks on supporting 4,000 of our most vulnerable customers. Everything from household assessments, curtains, insulation, heat pumps, practical stuff that helps homes. And as Mark said, we recognize the sector enables the wider economy, and that's why the issues that I mentioned to start with are so damn frustrating and challenging for everybody.

We do have one of the most renewable and secure electricity systems in the world, so we do have what we would frame as a longer term competitive advantage, which is good. And while prices are higher than we'd like, it's important to recognize that New Zealand currently ranks among the top 10 countries in the World Energy Council ratings for affordability.

So other countries are really struggling with similar challenges. We've also got more retailers per capita than you see in Australia and the UK, so levels of competition here are pretty sound, and we've got a market that works pretty damn well in our view. So in closing, I'd say the same thing that Mark did, is I want to acknowledge the efforts of people within our team that every day go out there and support customers, and are trying to tackle and overcome the structural challenge that I mentioned.

And I'm confident will prevail in time. But I'll leave it there because as Chair said, I know you've got plenty of questions. We have. Okay. I'm going to start with Carlos- Yes... then Megan, then Joseph, and yeah, we've been slow there. And then Simon, and then Scott. Okay. And we'll take about... And then Tangi. Okay. Right. Tangi, get ready.

You should give him- Yeah... and then I might ask some too. So we'll go around, but if we can have about up to four questions each, and then you get another crack. So there you go, Carlos. Yeah. So it's great to hear that you understand people are struggling, and also you've been mentioned that we are, New Zealand, are one of the top 10 country in affordable energy. But in your own terms, how do you define affordable electricity for ordinary households today?

And do you believe your current price meet the standard? When I talk to affordability, it's a relative measure. So, we think about that affordability challenge from an exporter perspective. So how does New Zealand support those who export and compete with companies that might be situated or located in Australia, the UK, the US, Asia? So when I talk to the World Energy Council ratings, I'm really talking about New Zealand remains affordable from a competitive perspective with those economies.

We know that people want cheaper electricity. There's no question about it. The costs of electricity have gone up as a result of gas, and we're not comfortable with the prices that we have seen, so we're comforted by the levels that prices have fallen to, wholesale prices that I mentioned, back to 120 bucks a megawatt hour, and we know that they are the competitive levels that you'll see.

So, from an affordability perspective, our job is to bring those prices back to that level. But do you think it's affordable? For those where it's not affordable, because there are people in our society that simply electricity is challenging, is that's where we bring our vulnerable customer program into play.

So for some people, it really does challenge them in going about their daily lives, and that's why we run that program. So firstly, how can you justify the profit level while many households are struggling to afford electricity? So this is one of the challenges that we have as a company. Obviously, as a business, you're only able to stay in business to the extent you're sustainable, and you must maintain profitability, otherwise you can't help customers, stakeholders, or anybody. So, our profitability is actually important.

We are aware that our profitability has... Well, it took a massive hit last year as a result of the conditions that played out. But as we restore to more normal levels, we do understand that people look at our profitability and question it.

But what I, again, when you look at the headline numbers, are large because we're a large business, is our rate of return in terms of profitability is really reasonable. It's mid-range single digits, so I'm talking our return on equity is around 5%.

Oh, it's just under. So we have to manage a trilemma of stakeholders, as I mentioned briefly before. Mm-hmm. And we are using shareholders' capital in order to invest, so we have to satisfy them. We're also conscious of satisfying customers, and it's actually not in our interest to have customers going broke or disappearing. Quite the contrary.

We wouldn't have a sustainable business either. So that's the balance that we have to maintain. When you're talking about households, the thing we tend to concentrate on is what is the proportion of someone's income that is being spent on energy. And I think if you looked at that over time, it's actually come down.

And related to that, the only additional thing that I would say is just remember, whilst we pass through the lines costs, we don't control that, and that's regulated. So we can deal with the bits that we deal with, which is retail and the generation margin.

So can you- Last question. Sorry. So if it's last question, all right. What would you say to working families in my school, in my electorate, who do not qualify for all the support from your company but still struggle to pay their power bills every month? Well, I would say get in contact with us.

But they do not qualify to your power support. So what are you going to say with those hard-working families in my school? We pride ourselves, every customer that we have that has challenges is we work with everybody. So that's why I say I'd encourage them to get in touch. If you've got families in your electorate that are really struggling, get them to talk to us about the challenges that they're facing so that we can make sure that they are supported by us.

Good. Megan. Thank you, and thank you for your presentation. First line of questioning I want to go on is around Pukaki. Probably no great surprise. In your written answers to the committee, you say that greater access to the contingent hydro resources which sit at the bottom of several South Island lakes would immediately reduce wholesale prices, provide a further buffer against dry year risk, and improve system efficiency and emissions outcomes.

So how central is to changing the rules for contingent storage at Pukaki to your strategy over the next decade, if you weigh that against investment and storage, demand response and new generation, and how will it impact on those things? We think it's really important for the country to have firming resources available. And of course, hydro reservoirs are fantastic at providing firming, and particularly as our gas reservoirs that are also reasonably flexible for the production and levels fall.

That's why we put the contingent storage to the fast track panel is, that firming ability of contingent storage will be important for the country. But you also picked up that right here, right now, and going back to the previous questions, Kiwis are finding the challenges with the cost of energy.

And we know that if you release that contingent storage, it will bring energy prices down. So the immediacy is driven by a reduction in wholesale prices, which will then flow through to retail prices over time. But in the long term, the country needs more firming resource, and that's what contingent storage provides.

So in your report, I think it is, that you estimate that $437 million of savings a year, how is this going to be passed through to households who are on fixed price contracts? Because as the EA and Transpower have pointed out, that that doesn't necessarily equate that that will flow through to households.

And what proportion of your book is hedged already and won't be seeing those three year downward pressures on prices that you're talking about? Where's the $437 million going to land? Yep. So it does take timeSo it does take time to filter through. Because there are two sorts of customers.

One is the commercial customers that they might run a one, two, three-year contract. As they come off contract, those contracts are then set against wholesale rates at the time that they enter into the contracts. And if wholesale prices are lower, they will get that benefit immediately when they re-contract. Say we were successful in realizing contingent storage release, as commercial customers coming off contract, the next day would see that benefit. For residential customers, your point is the next time that we look at pricing for

residential customers will be next April. And of course, if wholesale prices come off, then residential customers benefit from that when we look at those reviews. So in reality, households and businesses are not going to see downward pressure, not going to see a reduction in their power prices from this for quite some time.

They will absolutely see it. But it does depend on the length of contract that they're currently on. So the $437 million figure is accurate, but your point is, when does that flow through to customers? It will flow through in its entirety as we reprice residential customers and commercial contracts coming off.

Sure. But it could take up to three years for some hedge businesses. It could take time. Yep. So one of the things that your fast track application does is it effectively shifts 10% of the nation's storage, which is an incredibly valuable bit of our electricity network, from Transpower being the decision-maker to Meridian being the decision-maker around when to release this.

So what guardrails are you going to put in place in terms of the governance of that, so that this is operated in New Zealand's best interest and not in Meridian's best interest? Yep. When you look at 2024, as Mark said, the party that was exposed and saw a massive cost as a result of the gas market being challenged, as well as the hydro drought, was Meridian.

And Mercury. Yep. So we operate those lakes very, very conservatively and prudently. If anyone looked at how we operate them, they're operated more conservatively than ECNZ operated them. The proof is there. And we operate them more conservatively now than we did when we had both the Tekapo scheme and the remaining Waitaki scheme.

So I know that we operate those facilities in a very prudent way to manage both the risks of security, while making sure that consumers realize that benefit that we just talked to. As we are ultimately the ones that are exposed to dry year risk, and therefore, we treat this resource and its use very seriously.

So, I absolutely understand that, and there's no doubt that 2024 was an incredibly hard year for Meridian. But it was an incredibly hard year for the country. And we saw many businesses go to the wall. So our job as a parliament is to ensure that this asset is run in the best interest of New Zealand, not the Meridian balance sheet.

Agreed. To ensure that we are delivering those lower power prices. Now, there's a number of people have put up an argument that actually this could drive up our prices, where we get the perfect storm, that we have a dry year and an outage in the North Island of a major asset, that we have used our last line of defense at that point, and that we could see higher wholesale spikes on the spot market.

Yep. What is going to be put in place to protect New Zealand businesses from that being the case, and what governance arrangements will Meridian put in place? What we know, because we've seen the analysis and we've talked to everybody who's participated in that process, and we know that on average, there are benefits to Kiwis.

We put the numbers at about $400 million. Transpower puts them at about $40 million. And that the Electricity Authority, who has jurisdiction in this space, has looked at our analysis, Transpower's analysis, and gone, "On balance, Meridian is right that consumers will benefit." Even though there are sequences that are quite difficult for people, our regulator has looked at this challenge and said, "It is better to release contingent storage than to save it for the perfect storm," because the probability of that perfect storm playing out is incredibly low, even

though it could happen. And that would mean consumers would miss the entirety of the benefit while we waited for that perfect storm. And so on balance, everybody says, "Let's release the storage to market so that Kiwis who are struggling right now get the benefit of that water," because we know that water's cheap.

But we know that benefit is going to take some time to flow through, and we know that while the regulator has said this, that the system operator- Yep... Transpower, has raised some concerns- Yep... around the upward pressure on price this arrangement could have. So I think what would be really useful for the committee is before that fast track decision is made, whether all of Meridian's analysis around that could be released to the committee so that we could look at that.

Is there anything in particular that you'd like to see? Because we- So, yes. So what would happen in a scenario where we have dry year or cumulative dry years- Yep... coupled potentially with an outage in the North Island? Yeah. What are your concerns there? So this is the scenario that Transpower is raising. Yeah, so you'd like to see the- Any modeling that you've done around what the- You've got the sensitivity sure that- Yeah. Yeah, yeah.

Thank you. No, that's- I think on that we would ask some follow-up questions, certainly. Yeah, of course. The flow-through benefit- Yeah... from lower wholesale prices, that we want to understand how that's going to flow through, because everybody says the expectations come down. Yep. We want to start seeing notices saying your bill is coming down. Yep. That's a wonderful thing. And, yeah, and timelines.

Yeah. I'm going to go to Simon as a sup on that, just one sup, and then I'm going to Joseph, so that Joseph can then go and fare thee to the place. Well, Katie. Yeah. Thanks. I'm just interested in your fast track application for lowering Pukaki. You talk about needing to do it in order to do some rock armoring and some other engineering protection works in the event that you do end up taking it to that low level.

Will that involve draining the lake? So, the reason that we need to armor that dam face is when it was originally built, it was originally built to operate from 518 meters and up to 532, and the use of contingent storage would take it down to 513 meters possibly. The armoring of that 518 to 513 range, the rocks just aren't there.

Okay. And so what we've worked out, because we know that with droughts you could actually fall below the 518 range. So right now, under specific circumstances, we can use that contingent storage range. What we need to ensure is that the dam structure retains its integrity. So we've got a process that we can use to, as the lake falls, and it falls largely because of a drought. It's not that the water's going into, the Pukaki Canal, it's just that it's not raining.

But we know that if we need to make electricity to keep the lights on in the country, that we need to armor that face. Okay. And so what we would do is diggers would enter the current rock that sits on the dam face, and they would start placing rock at the lower reaches that weren't armored.

Okay. So. Would it make more sense from an engineering project management point of view to program that by making sure that the lake is low enough and do it all at once? Or is this something you're just going to do piecemeal depending on whether it rains or not? You can't. That's the- We, we- Yeah, yeah. Got you all. Yeah. It's really tricky, so we've looked at all sorts of ways to do it.

It's really tricky to do it when the lake level is above. Well, of course. But I don't think- Yeah, yeah. Si- So, well we'll let Joseph have the last say. I think you've got a pinch. Right. Joseph, and then I go to Simon. Oh, thank you very much, Chair, and, thank you very much for being here. It's a pure chance on here, actually, just having you cover a colleague, but I'm very pleased I am because this is a bit of a passion topic for me.

Manapouri's been my electric, been there twice. And White Hills Power wind farms. Also haven't been there yet, but I'm keen to. But look, on a first principles basis, I'm really interested in your thoughts about what we should do because renewable generation in this country is great, but it's only 55 to 60% of our generation.

We have a maximum of three months of hydro storage all going well, provided every other energy source holds up that we have for that other 40-plus percent of energy generation. That's one of the lowest rates in comparable countries in the world. Tasmania, for example, has more than 12 months of hydro storage, and they rely on the Basslink interconnector to Australia, and they had to rely on that back in 2024. And of course, in 2024, because they had dropping gas suppliers, central gas suppliers, prices peaked to $850 a megawatt

unit. Since then, I think it's dropped, was it 27% last year? 23%? That's yet to be questioned. Oh, I'm getting there, Mr. Chair. Okay. Because this is important. Yeah, no, we've got that all. They know that. 27 or 23% central gas. I think they understand that. But we have this massive drop. Germany, which has a high renewable energy penetration, has 11 connections to other countries but it's relying increasingly on France, which produces about 70% of its energy by nuclear.

Increasing on Poland- But this isn't a question. Come on, Joseph. I'm getting there, Mr. Chair. Yeah, well, you're taking a long time. No. You won't get us up. I'm getting there. This is important, Mr. Chair. You won't get us up. What- What are we doing? What can we realistically do, and urgently to... Because high power prices, we've got to reduce supply.

Supply and demand is going to go up. We need more generation, and a lot more. Yep. Yep. So, prior to 2024, the New Zealand electricity system solved largely, and solved at those 70 to 80 affordable levels because we had a wealth of hydro storage that worked really well, and we had an abundance of gas.

So when it stopped raining, we used the flexible cheap gas that we had. It was six bucks a gigajoule. It was really, really cheap, and the balance of those two products kept electricity prices really low. 2024 changed all of that. So we now do not have those gas reserves. supplies which is- Is the point you are making.

One. The first thing that we did- One.... to respond to that was we entered into the Huntly Strategic Energy Reserve to buy us time, so that gives us 10 years of flexibility, using coal to make electricity. How good. So it's a stopgap measure, but the country needed it to maintain electricity suppliesWhile we look at our hydro reservoirs . So we are looking at it.

You got one. Okay. All right. I'm going to go quickly. As the chair's given me one- I just quickly- So I'll go to like this If I could just add, there's a short-term issue, which we would say the industry has dealt with and urgently together, which takes us through to about 2028. And you will have heard from others around what they're doing around geothermal.

And similarly, we are investigating our ability to look at existing hydro assets and see whether we can actually bolster those to actually add. That's a longer-term solution, which won't happen overnight. So there's a short-term issue that had to be solved for, and we would say we're largely there.

And then there's the longer-term one, which everybody is working on very hard. No, no, no. I want to ask about Super El Niño, but we've got to- That's it. No. No. That's it. No, I don't. I just- Scott. Scott The last one. Just- No, no, no. No, no, no. No. No, you took too long. Scott. Okay. Scott. No, seriously. So, we've got to- I did say that.

So my question sort of follows on. We've got a really clear message from the government that they want fossil firming. Shh. And that's what's coming. This week. That's what we understand is coming. What- Too long... what fossil firming are you doing at the moment? So that's the Huntly- Yep... Huntly Reserve.

That's the only fossil firming you're doing. Yep. What are the options for you, Steve, with LNG for firming? Is that something that is interesting you in the future when you're getting through this tricky period? So no, we did an analysis on effectively have we managed dry year risk as an electricity sector, and what it shows us is that dry year risk has been managed through the series of agreements that we put in Huntly Strategic Reserve, the New Zealand Aluminium Smelter demand response agreement, and then some remaining generation sources.

So from everything that we can see, the analysis says dry year risk has been managed through the next 10 years. So, when we look at LNG, we go, "It's not necessary from an electricity sector perspective." And we're not saying the government shouldn't look at it because I talk to the gas challenge that's there, but that's an issue for the government, not for the electricity sector or for Meridian. What we're doing is, following up from Joseph's question, is we're looking at the resources that we have. We know that hydro reservoirs have not

been looked at or built for the last 60 years, largely because we've had access to abundant gas. We also know that hydro as a resource is really cheap to run. So if we had it, it would bring... Contingent storage is an example of the impact that it would have, but rebuilding your hydro storage is from a country perspective, brings long-term resilience. There's no question about it.

It just takes time to make the assessment of, is it economically feasible for the country and for us? So that's what we're looking at, and we know we've bought ourselves 10 years to both examine, and then if it makes sense to feasibly build it out. Okay. So that's really reassuring.

We've got 10 years without LNG, and I guess I'm thinking about the options for hydro firming, not just the access to the contingent storage. Yep. Could you go into a little bit of detail about what else you might be looking at for resilience for New Zealand Inc? Yeah. So we're looking specifically at Pukaki.

Pukaki is a modified hydro catchment, that facility. No one lives around the lake. I call it a natural bathtub, and so if you did raise it, it doesn't flood a whole lot of surrounding land like other catchments. So if you were going to look anywhere to expand your hydro storage, that's the place to look, so that's what we're doing. I don't have enough info- No... today to go, "Here's-"

Yeah. It's important to recognize this is early days. Yeah. We've got a hell of- Yes ... a lot of work to do, a lot of analysis- Yeah ... to be undertaken before we say, "That it's a happening thing." Yeah. No. Under- I understand that, and I appreciate the greatness. Yeah. I guess just a final question is, we've come through some dry years and have been managed, and you've taken some risk to hedge that.

Yep. We're now entering a Super El Niño period. What are your predictions for how that's going to affect the hydro system? Oh, it's really- If we knew that, we'd absolutely coin it at the moment. We would. In the wholesale market. Great answer.

Here's the interesting thing with El Niños is El Niño tends to bring more rain to- Yes... the southern hydro catchments on average. Yeah. La Niña is the pattern that tends to- Yeah... bring less. But the interesting thing about all predictions, Mark's point, is the two strongest rainfall years that we've had in the country happened during La Niña.

Mm. So, if you believe the forecast, there's a Super El Niño coming. That should be on average good for the country, because it'll mean more rainfall. But the reality is Mark's point, if we really knew the answer, I-We would be pretty happy.

One thing that's worth perhaps also mentioning in the context of resilience is there's a lot of renewable generation being built at the moment, and indeed the questions we get from our, for example, institutional investors is around, is there a risk of overbuild?

And yes, there is a risk of overbuild. And as a result of which, if that were to occur, then hydro, for example, would be used for firming in less instances. So that storage would be kept for longer. So that shouldn't be disregarded as a possibility.

Just one- Oh, okay. One last one... other one, then we'll go to Simon. Thanks. Yeah, so I'll just move to wind really quickly because you've got some wind assets from White Hill to the north. What is the strength? What is the value that you're seeing in that diversity of asset base in wind in particular?

Because are you noticing that the wind blows equally everywhere all at once, or does that diversity give us some resilience? The diversity gives us some resilience, but probably not as much as we originally thought. As the first point you made, Scott, is the wind either blows in most places or it doesn't, and interestingly, there's a correlation between a dry year, no rain, no storms, and limited wind.

Mm. So the actual complement that we're looking at to build out beyond the wind farms that we've got in our pipeline, and that currently exist, is how do we integrate solar into the system? Because when it ain't raining- Yeah... the other thing that happens is the sun's out. So solar as an asset in New Zealand actually brings complementary base that can sit alongside hydro and wind quite nicely as well.

Mm. Thank you. I'm interested in your opinion about the Transpower grid connection queue. We've seen Merian make comments in the past about bureaucratic delays. It takes two years on average from making an application just to commencing the engineering investigation, and that despite your intent to generation investment, the bottleneck is access to the grid. What's your opinion on how things are going now?

There's no question that Transpower's overwhelmed with the number of requests for grid connection. Mm. They've been nothing other than supportive of any of the projects that we've developed with them. They haven't slowed us down at all. The next three that we're looking at really carefully and hopefully bring to financial close either later this calendar year or early next calendar year, Transpower's supportive. So we know that there's a lot of people that are bringing their muscle to bear in New Zealand, given where wholesale prices have been.

People want to invest in generation assets and that has overwhelmed Transpower, but they have never slowed us down. Right. And as I say, we don't expect them to slow us down for the next few developments we've got either. They're getting quicker. Okay. Is that because your project development timeline kind of lines up with their time frames?

I think they're getting quicker. Is there no codependency or... No, when they look at their connection queue, they look at those projects that are funded and available, ready to go. Mm. And so they do... As I say, they've been a bit overwhelmed, and so they try and prioritize their time and attention, not just on us.

We don't get special treatment. It's just that if there's a developer that shows the intent, that has actually got board and shareholder commitment to funders, Transpower throw their whole support behind. What possibly helps is that when we look at projects or when the development team looks at projects, they will look at substation capacity and- Mm... those sorts of issues and the distance away from substations and the like.

So, it's inherently part of the process that we'll look at, to what extent can we help them help us? If I put it that way. Thank you. Yeah. Okay, I've got to grab a couple of questions then we're going to Megan. Thank you. And then to Katie. So just electricity costs around the country now, I know having two properties in two different places, one in Wellington, one in the Wairarapa, the difference in price is astronomical.

It's 50% more expensive in the Wairarapa than it is in Wellington. Yep. And that's really ouch. And I know the West Coast is the same. South Island too. There'll be a bunch of other places. Why have we got that bigger difference, and what can we do to bridge those kind of gaps that are for some of those regions? And I know we've got generation about to go in in the West Coast, but what helps to why- Line charges, yeah... and what helps to deal with that?

Line charges. So, yeah. Sorry, we were just discussing is you have 27 odd distribution companies around the country, and they all charge differently. So- And it's the energy price that's the difference as well, not just the line charge. The energy prices won't be that different.

Yes, they are. The only thing that should differ, it could be that you're on different products, because different products, if you've got a residential property in Wellington versus a farm, you might have a different product type, but the actual energy price that exists in the Wairarapa won't be that different to Wellington for the same product.Where we see the variation, because we have to run tariffs. We offer products to people- So there's no distance barrier is what you're saying?

No. Yeah, no. So when I talked to Transpower, they said that distance is an issue from generation. So if you're a long way from generation, if you actually had more generation, and I use the West Coast here as an example. Yep. And that's why they get excited by having the new hydro scheme- Yep... and other opportunities there, that it should pull prices down because that generation's close to the use.

Yes. You pay losses, so the closer you are to generation, the lower the loss between where it's generated and where you consume. That's what I see. And so that does- Yeah... factor into your price. But beyond the losses across the HVDC link from the South Island to the North Island, they aren't usually that substantial portion of... Other than, as you say, the West Coast.

Because interestingly, to get electricity to the West Coast, you have to take the transmission connection goes all the way up through Nelson and round the top of the country, South Island, and back down the West Coast, and so lines losses in the West Coast are significant. And it's one of the reasons that you might or might not have picked up, but Lightyear Solar is building a solar farm out that way, and we've supported them by writing a 10-year PPA- Mm... to actually help with West Coast electricity prices.

Okay. There'll be a follow-up question on that when we do our follow-up just to get some numbers around that. But the second area I just wanted to ask you about is gas- Mm... and the use of gas. So we've been using gas for electricity generation. Yeah. We've clearly got a bunch of industries who, and businesses and households that you can transition, but it's very expensive, and some of them can't transition at all.

How do we get ourselves in a position where we actually say we want gas to be going to those who actually need the gas as opposed to using it for electricity generation? What's your view on that? Because we had Genesis last week who said, households, businesses, electricity generation in that order. Yep. So the way we've historically allocated resources, gas or electricity, has been price. That's the way that- Well, my question is, is that the best way going forward for New Zealand Inc.?

Because electricity generators can always pay more than the gas users can. Yep. So we're well aware of the challenge that we've got with physical gas molecules and that's why arrangements like the Huntly Strategic Energy Reserve are so important because it means that the electricity sector uses coal, not gas, during a dry year.

Yeah. Which is where the real constraint emerges. So we are, while that framework of price determines allocation, we are building resources that means the electricity sector uses less gas over time. So every wind farm we build, every solar farm that we build means that more of the energy in the electricity sector comes from fuel types that mean that we don't use as much gas and more gas goes back to other electricity consumers. So while, I don't know whether there's a better mechanism than price, but we get it.

Like Mark said it before we want to grow the economy. We want people who are using gas to be able to convert to electricity or, if they don't have that conversion pathway, to have as lower price gas as possible. The- We also certainly think and I think there might have been announcements earlier this week around transparency in the gas market.

Mm. It's still very opaque. We've talked about that. It called us all out in 2024, as a really stark example. So anything that can be done to actually shed some light on how gas is being used and by whom and for what, has got to be a positive in our view.

Keep on that one. I won't ask my third question. I'll go to Megan. So related actually, and I think that just highlights how important having a gas transition plan for the country is. Look, one of the things I'm interested in the comments that you've made about LNG, you've talked about how while this is a levy on our electricity sector, you don't see a need for it in electricity. You think there potentially is a need for an industrial process fee for gas users.

Have you had discussions with government around where that levy should fall, whether it should be falling on electricity consumers, households, and businesses as a levy on their power bill, or whether it should be on the people who are really going to see the need and the benefit of it? We've been banging down as many doors as we possibly can to understand where the decision might go on LNG.

We know there's a procurement process underway. That's as much as we have for now. We don't know if that process will be completed, the procurement process for LNG or not. We understand that it's possibly July or August- Yeah... that the decision will be made, but- So one of the things that I asked Genesis- Probably as much as we've got... is last week, and probably in terms of those discussions ahead of the procurement process, have there been any discussions about, as a gentailer, around what an equity stake might look like, long-term capacity

contracts, fuel off takes, what that might look like? So we're not a fuel off-taker. No, but in terms of a- No... Huntly Firming Option kind of equivalent. No, we haven't been asked for equity. It's just too early. Yeah. There's a lot of detail to be worked out-Even potentially basic terms, it's just too early in the process.

Yeah. So ahead of any decisions being made, what we have, that you have your majority shareholder pushing a particular position in terms of a desire for that, have you sought any related party risk legal advice in terms of NZX rules, in terms of where that may sit in terms of the Crown as the majority shareholder, and them having a very clear policy position, and what that might mean if there are discussions around offtake agreements or capacity contracts or anything like

that with the electricity sector, or has your audit and risk committee considered this potential problem in terms of our NZX rules? Well, as I understand it, I'll just ask our general counsel. We don't have any advice on that yet. Is that something you might seek advice on?

Well, again- Given that you've got a... Obviously we're still waiting for decisions, but we do know that your majority shareholder, they're occupying that dual position of being a majority shareholder and the policymakers in terms of any decisions at a governance level that will need to be taken- Should be proud... that are commercial. Mm-hmm.

I knew somebody'd say that. It's just too early. Until you understand what potential proposals look like- Waiting for it... or any discussion around issues like that is entirely theoretical. Yeah. But you- Highly theoretical... you would say, as a chair of a board, that if it did come to a point where there were with the electricity sector, discussions around minimum offtake agreements, because we know LNG ships aren't just going to pull up, they're going to need to have offtake agreements. If there were any discussions with

MOM, with mixed ownership model companies, that there would have to be some provisions put in place around related party rules. Look, at the end of the day, our duty is to act in the interests of all shareholders, and that's what we'll do. Exactly. Mm-hmm. Exactly. Yep. Yeah. Okay. So, we'll keep asking that question about... And it's really your shareholder needs to be thinking about how they manage that conflict of interest.

Right. I'm going to go to Scott, Katie- Yeah... and then Simon. Scott. Yeah. Thank you. So coming back to the energy hardship question- Oh, Christian, do you want me to- I'm aware that you've got that program- Yep... to reach into homes. No, they're just explaining. And Genesis recently showing an interest in doing some work in the space as well. So I'm interested in any progress there might have been in joining forces or putting in a general fund from everyone, every gen tailer- Yeah... to have a more equitable distribution or a more efficient distribution of

energy hardship resources to those that we know are really doing it hard at the moment. And if not, is it something that we can progress? Because that's something I would be very keen to help progress. Yeah. Let's take away because... So the answer is not that we're aware of, Scott, some form of general fund.

But coming back to the questions asked around generally people struggling, we know. So we are thinking about our own commitments as it relates to our vulnerable customer program and what we might need to do in that space separately. But if you've got an interest or desire, please let us know, get in touch, and...

Okay. We- But just looking across the market, all the gen tailers seem to be doing- Yeah... if I could call it that. I'm not minimizing it, but something. We've all got initiatives underway from what I can see. Thank you. I get your point around- Yeah. Okay... how effective. Well, we... Yeah, thank. I'll follow up. Thanks, Scott.

Katie. Thank you. Jumping into this committee, but pleased to be here to talk to you. Obviously I'm from... Well, it might not be obvious to you, but I'm from Hawke's Bay. Recently had the opening of Patupaki, and then was just recently there representing the government for the golden row of Te Rahui, just on the other side of the boundary.

Really pleased about your comments about the diversification because, of course, as we've seen, if there's lots of rain, there's not a lot of sun. There's a lot of sun, there's not a lot of rain or wind. It works well. Just keen on your planning of battery storage, because just looking at your renewable development pipeline, you've got a lot of wind and solar coming in, but of course, as we know, especially with solar, when the sun's not shining, you're not generating.

There doesn't seem to be a good balance of battery and solar in that pipeline. And then I've got a follow-up question just about your community funding. So we're really proud of the storage we've got. We think we've got New Zealand's biggest batteries in the hydro lakes. Mm-hmm. Which is why we're looking at what can we do to get more from that storage because it's so... If you can get more water, it's cheap.

Yeah, wonderful. So you think you're underutilizing the batteries as it stands. You need more generation of it. Where we find batteries, you mentioned the battery energy storage systems to be valuable is there are times where, because the southern hydro lakes, big batteries are in the South Island, most demand's in the North Island, is you need those battery systems in the North Island to complement the transmission capacity across the HVDC link.

So when we look at battery systems in the North Island, we go, "Is there enough transmission capacity on the HVDC to use the big batteries?" If not, at periods of time, so if the North Island low peaks, do we need North Island batteries? So our battery at Ruakaka- Yep... is there to make sure that in those periods where there's not enough transmission capacity that-There's energy there in the North Island, and we actually just very recently received consent for a second energy park. And when I say energy park, it's a combined battery and-

Solar... solar farm, just out of Palmerston North. Brilliant. I see that Manawatu plan. Yeah. And then my second question, if that's okay, Chair- Yeah, yeah... is just around your community funding. So I know that there's the Power Up Harapaki. But just looking into it, the fund is a greater fund around the whole country. Do you have a commitment to making sure that that sort of is fairly allocated around those communities? Or is it just one central pool, even though you advertise it as a separate fund?

No, there's an allocation for each community. Right. So it's really... And we become part of a community- Yeah... as soon as we invest for the next 30, 40 years. And it's really important that we build a strong relationship and bring benefit in being part of that community. And so the community funds are a really key way of doing that.

And we bring some financial muscle, but we don't allocate the funds. So there's a community group that have full authority to use the funds that we will top up on an annual basis for each of the communities where we have our generation assets. So there's a fund for Harapaki, there's a fund for Ruakaka, there's a fund for the Waitaki. Wherever we've got generation, there's one here in Wellington for West Wind.

We're, to your point, we're really proud to support New Zealand's regional communities. Right. Thank you. Mr. Chair. Simon. I asked before about access to the Transpower group. Next question is around access to the distribution networks around the country. I've picked up some Meridian comments, primarily to the EA, that there's a wide variation in the order of costs between connecting to some networks around the country compared to others.

That the time taken to have the discussion and get the information you need is highly variable. Is there a risk that deploying batteries or other services becomes a postcode lottery, depending on how good the local distribution lines company is at actually getting on with it?

So where we've provided commentary on engagement with distribution companies- Yeah... for capacity on their networks has been our EV charging. Right. That's where we've- Yeah. Okay... really found that disparity in approach and cost. I suspect it's because EV charging is new.

And so, we might be the first party to go and talk to Ashburton Distribution company, and they may not have the process in place to deal with EV charging. And so for the most part, I think it's, we are forging a new path for many of the distribution companies because we find that when we go back for the first EV charger, it can be time-consuming, and it can be quite different, and the cost profile can be different from investing one in Christchurch.

But once you've educated somebody, and then they've got a process that they put in place, the second time you go back, there's an improvement in speed, there's an improvement in the way they think about cost. The third time... Because you need plenty of EV chargers in the same network. So, I think it's more about this is a network that's being rolled out for the first time, and so people are dealing with how they engage with us rather than there's a massive problem- All right... with disparity.

Mr. Chair, supplementary on that. Okay, you go on. I would've thought that if your business is running a lines company, that you would understand how to lay some duct and provide a connection and what amount of power is going to be drawn when someone plugs in a Tesla or a BYD.

Do some of these lines companies simply not have the capacity or the knowledge or the project management capability to be responsive? Because it's 2026, for goodness sakes. Good question to put to them. Yeah. Oh, I'm interested in your opinion. Again, you're right, they do have the capacity, they do have the knowledge to accept connections. It's just EV charging's new.

So it is new. We're under-represented as a country for EV charging. And so, we find that the first time we talk to many distribution companies, they haven't been through the process of connecting an EV charging installation. Okay. But as I say- Of course, we want them to prioritize us over everything and everybody else, but they'll have their own list of priorities.

And to be fair, we don't know what they are. They get good at it pretty quick. Yeah. I'm sure they do with you educating them. We're at our time, but Jonathan tells me that the next bit's going to be very, very quick, so we don't need 15 minutes for it. So if you're willing to stay for another 10, and colleagues- We're here at your pleasure... I'm seeing some enthusiasm for questions.

We will do that. So Tangi, you're next up. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Look, thank you. And I hear your comments earlier about an understanding that households are finding it extremely difficult at the moment. So if we look to the next three or five years, what is the cheapest form of generation that you can see us accessing or being able to provide?

A combination of solar and wind. When you look at the ability to build a wind farm or a solar farm, solar farms are quick, but they're not as efficient in making electricity. So, they have a conversion rate of about 20%. So the amount of energy coming from the sun, 20% of that is converted to electricity.

Whereas a wind farm, the capacity factor, you get the wind blowing, it's more like 40%. And if you look across the sector, that's where the biggest amount of investment is going in at the moment. I'm from the Manawa too, so I appreciate that. But are you confident that you're doing all that you can to tap into those opportunities?

There's no question. We're going as fast as we possibly can. What was holding us back, if I just use some numbers for people, is over the last 10 years the electricity sector's invested $12 billion in new generation. Over the last five, it's $6.5 billion.

We're slowed down by consenting only. Now, the government's introduced fast-track legislation that has made a hell of a difference. It really has. To just come back to the lift in costs and electricity, driven by that structural issue that I mentioned.

We feel terrible about it, because if we could build this new energy faster, we know the cost would've come down quicker. So the introduction of fast-track legislation to help us consent these assets quicker has been brilliant from our perspective.

I think some of us are enjoying hearing that. Okay. We'll go for Sa. Yeah. Okay. So just on that, and going back to my original line of questioning. In your internal planning and guidance that you're giving to analysts and investors to the market. So is the Pukaki, if that goes through, is that effect built into your base case, EBIT and dividend forecasts, or is it treated as an upside scenario that only applies if the consent is granted on the terms you want?

How are you treating it in terms of your market information? So, we would only count it if the consent is granted. The interesting thing with contingent storage, and we've been quite open to this, is the benefits flow to consumers. But that's not how market analysts are treating it. They're seeing it flow through- No... to EBIT and to dividends.

No. Not contingent storage. There's no material uplift to Meridian's earnings as a result of contingent storage release. It's the only thing that we can do as a country to increase the amount of stored energy, stored hydro, which then flows into price, and we can do it at the stroke of a pen. It can be quick, and it provides relief the fastest, even though your point, it flows through to customers based on their contract period.

But we know the alternate is you've got to build a wind farm, and it takes a couple of years to get that wind farm in place. Yeah. And I don't think that's the question I'm asking. Yeah. It's more about the benefit to Meridian versus the benefit to New Zealand. Yep. And where this is going to sit in terms of your balance sheet. We know 2024 was a terrible year for you. Yep. And you said it, that you hedged before it rained, and so access to the contingent storage would do that, and I think this is coming full circle. We need to know when is it that consumers, households, and

businesses are going to see those lower power bills rather than it being something that can smooth out the Meridian balance sheet- Yep... and be a benefit to Meridian. But we can get that kind of from you. I think we will certainly have a follow-up question just asking for that trajectory of what you see- Sure... happening with power prices going forward.

Yeah. Possibly also going backwards as well, but also maybe profitability as well. I think that comes in there. Yeah. Really happy to. But just that contingent storage release is driven for the benefit of consumers. Yeah. But I think the question, Mike, what we're saying is we want to know when is it going to flow through to households and businesses-... because we're seeing a three to five-year lag.

Totally. Yeah. Totally understand. I'm going to give myself one. Did you have one, Scott? Yes. Okay, I'll give myself one, then I've got Carlos and then Scott, and you can wrap them up, Scott. Just my one, you mentioned in hydro, the only thing you're actively looking at in the early days yet you said was Pukaki. I don't know if you've had any engagement with the folks Great South, but they've got a whole electricity, and energy strategy where I'm very impressed actually.

I don't think any other region's even close to them on that score. But they're very interested in hydro opportunities that there are in the southern part, the south island. They're run of river. Some of which are in your areas. A lot of them are run of river. But I just wondered if you've been thinking about them, and obviously you had it going back a while, the efforts on Project Aqua in Waitaki. But are there some opportunities potentially at some stage to say we could do more with some of those areas where the rivers are significantly modified already? I'm not talking about Mokonui.

Yeah, okay. Well, if there's anyone out there that... Because we're not talking to Great South. So if there's anyone that you know of, please send them our way. The reason we're focused on Pukaki is the scale of support that gas provided to the new energy system, both electricity and gas, was massive. So for us to play a proper role is we need to look at hydro storage and scale hydro storage, and that's why we're focused on Pukaki, is it's New Zealand's biggest battery already, and if we can enlarge it in one capital project, it's better than doing it

across five smaller programs. I hear what you're saying, yeah. But if you've got someone- I'll send them in your direction. That'd be great. They presented at the Murahuku wānanga yesterday, and your team was there. Right. Okay. There we go. Yeah. So they'll be aware. They're more than aware. They are. Yeah. They've done some very impressive work. There you go. Carlos and then, Scott, wrap up.

Thank you. I'm going to go to 10 past, and then we're going to flip to the next item. Right. Very quick question. Do you believe the current electricity market fulfill a fair paying field for smaller power company? Yeah. Absolutely. The wholesale market, doesn't matter who you are, whether you're big or small, is You get paid the same amount whether you generate a huge amount of energy or a small amount of energy at the same node. It makes no distinction between size. None.

But if you think they are fair, how come we heard a little feedback from small energy retail-- small power company, they've been mentioned that the paying field was very unfair to them. It is much harder for them to access to the infrastructure, the to the market.

How are you response to this? I think you're talking about retailers rather than generators, but okay. Yeah, both. Both. Yeah. Okay. Well, if you can answer that anyway. Level playing field. Yeah. So, we've always felt that we treat our retail business the same way as we treat independent retail companies, but they haven't felt the same way.

So, the Electricity Authority adopting the level playing field regulations are a good thing from our perspective because they will now ensure and provide the evidence that we do that. So, we're supportive of what the Electricity Authority has done in that regard. We've always felt that we treat people equally, whether you're large or small.

But those rules that have been introduced will now prove that that's the case, so I think it's good- It's probably worth clarifying, the EA has, to my knowledge, has never said, "We've found an issue that needs to be addressed," but they've put these rules in place, and so we're all in favor of that. If there's more evidence that's required to just show there isn't an issue, then that's great.

Okay. I'm going to go. Scott, you've probably got time for about one or maybe two if you're lucky. Final question. We talked about social license, and you mentioned fast track enabling the build, but fast track's also come up against some areas and some problems because it's overridden social license. So, the social license question is really about how do we improve that? Because we know we need the massive build.

One of the proposals that's been floated for some time is the community dividend, not just paying out, but enabling community buy into generation to essentially purchase a turbine or a parcel of water or something so that over time, while it might be managed by Meridian, the returns are regular to the community and they feel like they are part of that generation project.

Is this something that you've been open to and have looked at or would look at? So we'd be open to it. What we tend to do-- So before we go through fast track or any consenting process, we want to run an extensive program with local community so that we understand people who support and those people that don't support our programs, and we work with them before we'll submit our application for consent, because we know that that actually helps deal with a number of the issues.

We haven't run into anybody that said, "We want to buy a turbine off you or get proceeds. You own the wind farm or solar farm, but we want proceeds from, say, one or energy from one to date." But we would look at, of course. So it might require a legislative change to make that a requirement or...

I'm not sure it would. No, I think we'd consider it. That's the key point. What we've tended to do is, if we're putting it on farmland, is we'll enter agreements with the farmers for revenue from the turbines. But we've never had that request of, "We like that turbine. We would like to be seen to take energy from that turbine." If someone came to us and said, "That's the contribution we would like as part of this community,"

yeah, we'd be up for it. And on that collaborative note, so thank you very much. Thank you. It's been great to have you here for answering all the questions. We will send some follow-up questions, not just to you, but we send follow-up questions to lots of organizations. And you'll definitely get one back with the trajectories of price and profit and so on. Thank you. So really appreciate that. Colleagues, thank you.
